sentences.text: Yeah, it is the 10 Kilometer Camp.
No other name.
Just 10 Kilometers-- that's it.
Yes.
I sent-- I sent a substantial amount of cards.
We were allowed after half a year in that camp.
Now, we would-- let me retract that a little bit.
In that camp, we were accused of being German spies.
Simple as that-- we were accused of being German spies because we were in a territory where we didn't belong to.
And the Russians simply accused us of being German spies, and that's the reason we found ourselves where we were.
So in 19-- about a half a year later, they retracted.
They didn't accuse us anymore of spying.
And the situation improved in that camp as far as the food was concerned and as far as friendliness.
And that was people, you know, like the KBG [INAUDIBLE] there.
There was no KBG then, [INAUDIBLE] people-- were smiling, but nevertheless the direct-- the camp director by the name of Smirnoff-- I don't even-- Smirnoff was his name.
He still say, forget about going home nevertheless.
That's your home town.
You're going to build it here.
He still insisted on that.
But anyway, there were smiles, and we were able to get a newspaper from the town.
People-- when you were a good worker, they relieved you.
You could have gone sort of like for a day-- you had to be back in camp the same day-- to a town to get yourself a newspaper if you wish, or something else.
We didn't get paid, so we didn't have any moneys there.
But you could go.
Didn't cost you any money.
You got just that piece of paper, and you went to the station.
You got a ticket, and you went to the town.
But nevertheless, about half an-- about half a year later, six months later, situation improved.
And we were able to write.
We were allowed to write home to our homes.
Strictly postal cards.
No letters.
Letters were not permitted, just a postcard.
We received postal cards from the KBG people, and we sent postcards.
And I sent a couple of them home.
I don't remember exactly how many.
But I sent a couple cards home.
And my uncle sent also home, and he also sent to his brother, which was left in Lwow.
One of his brother was left in Lwow because the Russian didn't allow him to move out there because they needed him.
He was an accountant, and they needed accountants.
So they left them there.
So he got a couple of those cards from his brother.
And in one of those cards-- it went through.
I don't know how it went through-- he says, I wish I would be there with you.
Because they knew more than we did.
So I wish I would-- that was, you know, in the beginning of the 1941.
I, myself, was-- was sent couple card to my mother.
And I did receive two cards.
I did receive-- one came in February.
The other one, I don't remember, came the same month or-- or-- a week later, or a week after in March.
I don't remember exact.
And I send the card.
And, of course, you always send cards from a situation like that-- you're doing fine and smiling cards.
We're doing great, and everything else.
How are you people doing, and so forth.
Just strictly like that.
Anything else wouldn't go through.
So in one of my postal cards-- in the second postal cards, my mother asked me, would you like to have, you know, any packages from me?
I said no.
You know-- I would like to send you a package.
That's the way she said.
I would like to send you a package.
Tell me what you need.
OK?
I knew that the package will never come to me, and I didn't want to scare her to death that I really need this stuff, which I needed very, very badly.
So I simply said, no, we have everything here.
We're doing fine.
I'm working.
I'm getting fine.
I'm feeding myself good.
No, please don't send me any packages.
And that was the last postal card which I had.
My mother, of course, described, you know, about the kids and about the rest of the families.
They're doing fine.
They were not disturbed about anything yet in 1941.
Because there was no war between Russia and-- and Germany then.
So everything was moving normal.
So that was the only thing which she sent-- you know, something like that.
It could have been that she sent more postal cards and didn't go through the censors.
Could very well be, because the Germans had censors, and the Russians had censors.
So it could very well be that.
But I received two postal cards, and I was glad to get those two postal cards.
How many she did receive, I don't know.
Now, did you hear in Siberia about Germany attacking Russia--
Yes.
Yes.
We heard-- we heard from the KBG people.
As a matter of fact, there was one big bullhorn, and it was announced.
It was not announced directly on that particular day.
I think it was announced maybe five days later-- you didn't get anything from the same day-- that Russia was attacked to-- by the-- by the Germans.
And, of course, then it came out new lectures that now, really because of that, we really need that wood.
Work harder.
And we would be working harder, you will be rewarded for it.
And this and that, because the country has to survive.
And, you know, all those political statements.
But anyway, that's the only time which we heard about the war.
And after that, of course, when the news were good, we knew about it because we were told.
When the news were bad, we didn't know anything what was going on.
We didn't know that Russia was occupied so fast in the beginning of the war.
There was absolutely no knowledge about that.
Nobody told us anything.
So we were in the dark.
And even-- I don't even know if the Russian people themselves knew, because the newspapers didn't reveal either.
Because some people went to town, you know, on that pass.
They didn't bring any news that everything is bad or something like that, because the newspapers wouldn't print it.
So we didn't know.
But as soon as something started to get good, everybody knew.
It was through the bullhorn and everything.
As a matter of fact, sometimes they even connected the radio to let the people know how great they're doing, so-- but until that point, there was absolute blackout.
We didn't know anything.
We just didn't know.
OK.
Eventually-- that eventually-- that-- the eventuality came in 1941 right after the United States and Britain ended the Nonaggression Pact with the Soviet Union.
That was in 1941 after the war started.
I don't recall the month, believe it or not.
I don't recall the month.
In any event, right in that-- in that agreement, because they were close to 2 million Poles there-- there were about 145,000, maybe less Jewish people.
But the rest were all Poles.
And they had people there which were interned in the prison of war camps.
And the Poles-- which the same thing from the area.
Because they-- they resettled people.
Those people were living in Lwow before, and they were resettled and brought in which-- with their own Russian people to that territory.
So they resettled up close to 2 million people there from all over.
People would run away.
People which were caught as POWs, and people which was resettled from the area.
They resettled-- most of the people were sent out from the Ukraine and from the white Russia.
And that was Bialystok-- you know, that area there.
Those people were resettled.
The Polish people, the same thing.
And they started mostly with the intelligentsia, you know, something like that.
But they were close to-- my estimation is it was close to 2 million people.
There are a lot of people.
Even so, I don't know.
No.
Polish Jews--
--and Poles.
Polish Jews and Poles.
No, there was-- I don't know, maybe 35% Jewish people, and the rest were Polish people.
We were all together.
Oh, yes.
Everyone was Polish.
Everyone was Polish.
Nothing-- nothing from different countries.
Because you had people from Czechoslovakia, something like that.
Maybe there was a family or two which-- I don't remember-- from Czechoslovakia or something like that.
But the rest were all from Poland-- from Poland.
No problem with that.
First of all, you know-- it was very strange those days.
Now you have anti-Semitism in Russia just as well as any place else.
Those days, there was no such thing like anti-Semitism in Russia.
There was no such thing.
Because they had-- you know, if somebody was called somebody Jew-- Jew in Russian was iudey.
If you called somebody iudey or spelt out any other nationality-- if you were Tartar, or whatever you were, you were put away.
It was so strict against-- against-- this is one country, they said, and that's it.
So no, there was no anti-Semitism-- from the Polish people themselves, we didn't have any anti-Semitic because they were in the same boat.
And secondly, maybe there were some, you know, but then they were afraid of the KBGs.
And everybody was under the blanket.
As simple as that.
I mean, you know-- even-- you know, that's the way it was.
How they spoke--
--between themselves, I don't know.
But that's the way I saw it.
At this point, then, the agreement was with the United States that all the Polish people--
All the Polish people--
--should go free.
And they were let free.
Myself, I-- we got free in like November of 1942.
November ninth-- ninth-- no, November, 1941.
I'm sorry.
November, 1941.
But by the time it reached our area, that was-- I mean, we were free before that, but we just couldn't decided where we should go.
We don't know the country.
Some people didn't-- I was lucky.
You know, I was young, and I pick up the language fluent.
So I had no problem with the language, but some people didn't.
You didn't have any map, because a map-- you got caught with a map, and that was a crime.
That was spying.
There was no maps in Soviet Union.
Forget about it.
Just the military had the maps.
And so we had no idea.
We heard about town on the Volga River.
And it, believe it or not, was a German republic.
We know the language, so we wanted to go to that republic.
It was right on the Volga River.
It was right between Saratov and Kolpashev, and the name of the republic was the German Republic.
And the main city was Engel's.
So we wanted to go there.
So that was our aim-- to get to Kolpashev, to Saratov, from Saratov to Engel's.
That was our aim.
And we got there.
We got there.
And we got there, and the town was completely empty.
There was about 1.5 million German-- in that-- German people, but born in Russia from way back.
There were about 1.5 million living there-- beautiful city and everything.
But because we didn't know, you know-- we didn't know anything about the front line, how far it is, because we didn't have any maps.
Myself, my future wife, my uncle, and his wife, and the other-- the other sister-in-law, and a brother-in-law, another little baby at that time.
That baby was born in September 1940, so at that time he was already one-year-old-- David.
And that was the group of people.
That was the we.
We traveled together to the end.
Except when I went-- when I entered the army, I was on myself.
One of my brothers also was called.
I volunteered.
He was called in, because a wife and a child-- you don't volunteer to go to the army.
You wait till they called in--
--and he was called in.
--we'll go back to Engel's.
Very good.
We got to that empty city, and we found our government office.
And we went in and said that we are here.
What can we do?
He says, you cannot do too much here because there's nobody here, but you can sleep over in any place you wish and then see what you can do the following day.
There was nothing there, so we decided to go to a different town.
You couldn't go to Kolpashev, because Kolpashev was already there.
The Soviet government was already planning to have the capital city there.
In other words, not the capital city but the main headquarters in Kolpashev.
That was a restricted area, so we couldn't go there.
So we took the train to a-- to a town, a small town by the name of Krasnyi Iar, which was not far away-- about 50 kilometers away.
And we got there.
And once you get there, you have to start working.
In Russia, you don't sit.
You have to work.
[LAUGHS] And we needed the money anyway.
That was already for money.
That was our bread.
That was our money.
And anyway, I had-- I had no problem finding a job in the-- in a tailor shop.
And one of my uncles was also a tailor, so there was no problem there.
My brother, on the other hand, he was a businessman, so he didn't have any trade.
So he got a job in transportation-- that means horses.
[LAUGHS] And that was it.
And I don't remember if my wife was called in to work also in something.
I don't remember that.
But in any event, that's what we were doing there.
And very strange-- we had a small house-- small house.
And at that time, we didn't know the condition from-- first of all, we didn't know from what this house was built.
And one night, myself and my brother came back from work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the way, that brother now is my uncle, so that will come out later.
[LAUGHS] In any event, we came back from work, and everybody was asleep in that house.
And the house was full of gas.
There was no gas in that house.
But the house was-- was built-- believe it or not, the house was built from bricks.
But the brick was made from cow manure.
And the condition where it was wintertime, and you close all the-- you know, in winter, you close the windows, OK?
We didn't know that.
But in those particular houses, you're not supposed to have closed the windows.
You're supposed to have left the windows open, because the heat exposed and somehow-- somehow developed gas from that thing, from the outside.
And they were half dead.
The whole family was half dead.
We ran to neighbors, and the neighbors called the-- the ambulance, whatever it is.
The ambulances came in, and they dragged them out.
First of all, everybody-- we walk out, and out of the house to get some fresh air.
And that's the only thing which saved them.
Then they brought them to the ambulances, and they took them to the hospital.
They survived.
They survived, but we almost died.
So-- [SIGHS]
Now, there was another reason which we-- choose that-- I'm sorry.
I forgot that the main reason, actually, which we choose that-- that Engel's there, and Kolpashev, and Saratov.
Because at that time, we heard that the Polish army is being organized in that area.
OK?
And that was our aim-- to get with the Polish army.
Because we-- at that time, we knew that the Polish army takes the families.
Wherever the army goes, the family comes along.
So that was the reason for us to go to Engel's, to be close to Kolpashev.
But as-- the same thing because the front line was approaching, that army was moved out.
That army was moved out in September or August and was moved out to Central Asia-- to Central Asia.
Central Asia-- that means the-- the main city's Tashkent, and then the surrounding areas-- Samarkand, Namangan-- that's where that army was, closer to the Iranian border.
Closer to the Iranian border.
In any event, so we stayed there in that-- in that-- Krasnyi Iar, and that's where we found out that the army was moved.
So, of course, we would have stayed longer because the jobs were good and the pay was all right.
Unfortunately, when these incidents occur, we didn't want to stay there.
So we found out where the army is, and they told us, It is in Central Asia.
We had no problem of getting a ticket, because they didn't cost us any money from the camp itself.
We had our special paper.
You go.
There was no provisions on the train.
They didn't give you any food or something.
They didn't give you any provision of what to do-- what to-- where you should go.
But if you want to go to Central Asia, that's fine.
Whenever there is available train, you can go.
So we went to Central Asia from that thing.
After that incident, we stayed not a couple of weeks.
And we immediately decided when we discovered that the army moved, we went to pursue it.
So we pursued that thing here to Central Asia with the trains.
They were the same thing.
You didn't have any passenger trains.
You had-- few ra-- [INAUDIBLE] passenger trains, and then-- then were radio transport trains.
But it was not, you know, secluded, and neither was-- they had already facilities there, and the doors were not shut.
You didn't have to beg the KBG guy to open up, give his water, something.
You were free.
You didn't have any provision.
Was on your own.
We got provisions strictly-- not for money.
For money, they didn't want to have any money.
The peasants did not want any money.
They didn't have nothing to do with the money.
They couldn't buy anything for the money.
So you traded things.
You traded things.
The business commodity was like matches, tobacco-- none of them-- none of those people smoked at that time.
Tobacco, matches, and salt, and, of course, clothing.
So whatever we had, we traded.
And that's how we got the bread, and water, and whatever it is.
So the trip itself to Tashkent took close to two weeks-- close to two weeks.

And in one of those nights when we went for trading-- usually they-- the-- the conductor, whoever it was, that announced that the-- or the engineer announced, the train would stop here for two hours, refueling, whatever it-- or three hours, sometimes even longer.
So this time, they announced that the-- the train would stop for two hours, or two and 1/2 hours.
And that's it.
So we went for two and a half hours, myself and my-- myself and my brother not to trade things in.
Myself and my brother-in-law to trade in.
Meanwhile, we got back.
The train was gone.
The train was gone.
Now, we had-- each train had a railroad-- had a-- a Red Cross car.
And they had special markings.
So that-- that was our-- you know, there was so many trains on this thing here.
Because when they stopped, they stop always on a large railroad station.
They had so many trains, and they all looked the same.
They all looked the same.
Anyway, we got back.
The train was not there.
So we look for another train with a Red Cross car.
And we knew that everything is going in that direction.
So we simply hop in on that train-- on one of the-- on the open train.
On the open car.
And they were-- they were shipping things-- factory things, which they had resettled from the east to that area.
So we jump in on one of that train, and we're driving the whole night.
We almost froze to death, because you drive to the-- through Kazakhstan.
There's nothing there, just open, open space for three days.
And it's bitter cold.
It's hot like I don't know what during the day, but it's bitter cold during the night.
We didn't have any clothing, just a shirt and whatever it is.
In any event, we were very lucky.
We-- the train stopped daytime, because night there was no use to go out, because you couldn't do anything.
We were very lucky.
The train stopped, and we started to look for our train.
And we knock on one of the doors which we thought that's what it is, and sure enough, it was our-- our-- our [LAUGHS] railroad car.
So we came in, and we survived that thing here.
Because people got lost for the rest of the war and like this.
So we were very lucky.
And anyway, we got some goods.
[LAUGHS] And we were pursuing it again.
And it happened the same thing on the next station.
Of course, we were more careful and didn't go away for a long time, not too far away, because we needed food.
We needed food.
We had a baby, and we had people there.
So we always changed.
Sometimes I went.
Sometimes my uncle went.
You know, that's the way it was done.
And finally, we got to Tashkent.
After a tremendous amount of time and misery, we got to Tashkent.
We came to Tashkent, and there were millions of people.
People who were sleeping outside on the streets.
You had dead people.
You had alive people.
You had sick people with typhoids, with all kinds of diseases.
Because you have to remember that Tashkent is very, very hot.
It's 110 degrees, 120 degrees-- you know, the heat.
And the-- the people cannot take a bath.
They cannot take a shower.
They cannot anything.
You develop all kind of diseases-- malnutrition and everything, because people were starving to death.
There was no food there.
There was no food there.
There were registrations to go to a farm, to work on a farm.
And, of course, wherever there is something to work, you register.
So we registered.
And we were lucky to find a farm near Namangan, and the place was [?
Kim ?] Kolkhoz.
Kim was the name.
And Kolkhoz, of course, is the-- the collective.
That's the name of-- for the farm, collective.
And that was it.
So we got there.
And, of course, as far as farming, they were growing cotton there.
Their main produce was cotton.
And, of course, they had a lot of fruit trees, you know, like peaches.
And, you know, the all kind of the-- the fruit which-- which grows in the south, they had it there.
They had it there.
Very good quality, as a matter of fact.
And they also grew rice and regular farming.
Now, farming, you couldn't work during the day because it was too hot.
So farming started very early in the morning, like 4 o'clock in the morning.
And you stayed there for about four hours or five hours-- depends on the day.
Then you went home, and you resume your activities again in the evening when the sun went down, again staying there.
Myself, I was not farming there.
I was doing work with the horses and camels.
They had camels, and they had horses.
So I was feeding those animals, and taking care of them, and cleaning them, and bringing them to the-- to the water.
Can you imagine me carrying a-- I mean, drawing a-- an-- not elephant, a camel to water?
And anyway, that's what we were doing.
And some people were-- were doing the-- working on cotton.
Some people were helping.
They have-- they had silk factories.
Not silk factories, but natural silk.
They had those spiders which were producing silk, believe it or not.
And some people were working with that.
And everybody had some kind of a job.
As far as quarters, the same thing.
We get to the farm, they had more people than they needed.
But the manager of that farm was a Uzbek.
Those are Uzbeks.
Uzbeks believe, you know, they're Muhammads.
And they believe in Allah, you know, as their God.
And, of course, they did not like the Russians.
They did not like the Russians.
The Russian did very bad things with him after the-- when they took over that territory, and they didn't like the Russians.
So they find out that we are from Poland, and we are Jewish.
And they found out also that we don't like to eat pigs, so that was fine.
They had to grow pigs, but we had to take care of the pigs, so.
When they saw a pig on this side of the street, they cross over.
They were worse even than us.
But they had to grow pigs because the government asked them to.
They had to have a quota of each animal to be grown-- pigs, and sheeps, and, of course, horses.
Horses they needed for one reason.
Because the horses-- they use the horses for the cavalry.
Those are special horses.
And as a matter of fact, if one of those people got recruited to the army, OK, he went along with that horse.
He got the horse from the-- from the-- from the-- place.
He got the uniform and the horse.
And the gun he got when he entered the army, but the horse went along with him.
They were very good horsemen.
So in any event, we were working there.
And as far as quarters, as I mentioned, we didn't get any special houses, something like that.
And because we were from Poland, and because we were Jewish, the-- the manager of that farm give us their office space.
In other words, he took part of his office, and he gave us a room.
And, you know, provided with a kitchen or something like that.
I don't remember how we cooked.
Or we cooked outside or something like that.
I don't remember.
But in any event, we get a room to sleep.
There was no furniture in there, so we improvise.
We ask him to give us a word or doors for beds.
And we put them on brick or on stone, you know, away from the floor.
And we spread those things, you know, like one long bed.
Put straw on it, because there were-- there was no mattresses, and put whatever we had on top of it as a sheet.
And that was it.
That was our mattress.
And nevertheless, we were able to stay there.
Yes.
We found it.
We found it as soon as we settled.
We pursued the Polish army.
They were only about it 100-- not 100, about 75 kilometers away, which is not a big distance.
Thank you.
